title: St. Cyril of Jerusalem, St. Sinuthius of Atripe, St. Benedict of Nursia & St. Euphrasia of Tabennisi
narrative:
St. Cyril of Jerusalem, St. Sinuthius the Archimandrite of Atripe, St. Benedict of Nursia, and St. Euphrasia of Tabennisi

On the fourteenth of Megabit, the holy Church gathers four figures whose lives together constitute a meditation on the Rule — on the principles of order, discipline, and communal formation that govern the monastic and catechetical life. Saint Cyril of Jerusalem, who delivered the great series of Catechetical Lectures to the candidates for baptism in the Church of the Holy Sepulchre during the fourth century, is the supreme teacher of the Lenten catechumenate: his twenty-four lectures, delivered during the weeks before Easter, walk the candidates through the entire architecture of Christian faith from the meaning of repentance through the articles of the Creed to the mysteries of baptism, chrism, and the Eucharist. His work is the most complete surviving account of how the early Church prepared adults for initiation, and it demonstrates that the Lenten season was originally designed not as a season of private penance but as a time of intense communal theological formation.

Saint Sinuthius — Shenoute of Atripe, the great archimandrite of White Monastery — is the most commanding figure in the history of Egyptian Coptic monasticism: a man who governed his monastery and its associated communities of thousands of monks and nuns for more than eight decades with an authority so absolute, a discipline so strict, and a literary output so vast that he dominates the entire subsequent history of Coptic culture. His Coptic writings — sermons, letters, canons, theological treatises — are the foundation of Coptic literary culture, and his fierce pastoral care for the poor of the Nile valley, his confrontations with powerful landowners who oppressed them, and his destruction of the last pagan temple in his region make him a figure who combined the contemplative and the prophetic in a way that recalls the greatest of the Hebrew prophets. The Ethiopian church, which received its monastic formation in part through Coptic channels, honours Shenoute as a father.

Saint Benedict of Nursia is the father of Western monasticism, whose Rule of seventy-three brief chapters became the governing document of nearly all monastic life in the Latin West. The Ethiopian Church's encounter with Benedict is primarily through the witness of those Western missionaries and scholars who came to Ethiopia in later centuries and who carried with them the Benedictine tradition of learning, hospitality, and ordered prayer. But the Rule itself, when read alongside the Pachomian and Shenoutian rules of the Egyptian tradition, shows remarkable convergences: the same insistence on humility, the same ordering of the day around the hours of prayer, the same conviction that the community's life together is a school for the service of God.

Saint Euphrasia of Tabennisi is this day's figure of discovery: a noblewoman of the highest Byzantine aristocracy who, as a young girl, persuaded her widowed mother to bring her to a monastery in the Thebaid rather than to accept the imperial marriage that had been arranged for her. The abbess, testing her seriousness, assigned her the most degrading tasks of the community — carrying rocks, cleaning latrines, serving the most difficult sisters — and Euphrasia performed each task with increasing joy, recognising in the humiliations of obedience the shape of the freedom she had been seeking. She lived in the monastery for thirty years, dying young but complete. Her story is the female version of Benedict's own journey: the aristocrat who found in voluntary lowliness the dignity that involuntary privilege had always denied him. Amen.
